serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of flavoured liqueur is approximately one shot glass (30 ml / ~33 g), typically consumed neat, over ice, or as a cocktail ingredient.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured liqueur — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured liqueur (cr_id D01040001). Energy 53 kcal/100g, carbohydrate 13.12 g/100g, protein 0 g, fat 0 g, sodium 2 mg confirmed from HPB record. Note: the sugar field reports 0 g which is anomalous for a liqueur; the carbohydrate value of 13.12 g/100g likely represents residual sugars. This is preserved as reported by HPB with a caveat noted.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for generic flavoured liqueur in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sweet alcoholic beverages with comparable carbohydrate content (~13 g/100g). Alcohol itself has a GI of approximately 0 (does not raise blood glucose directly) but the residual sugar/carbohydrate component drives a moderate GI estimate. GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (13.12 g/100g × 33 g serving = 4.33 g) / 100 = 2.8. This is an estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content — direct pressor effect and antihypertensive drug interaction
hypertension.guidance: Flavoured liqueur is an alcoholic beverage; alcohol directly raises blood pressure through sympathetic activation and renin-angiotensin stimulation, and it interacts with antihypertensive medications — individuals with hypertension should avoid all alcoholic beverages including liqueurs.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol-induced hypoglycaemia risk and residual carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Alcohol impairs hepatic gluconeogenesis and can precipitate hypoglycaemia in patients on insulin or sulfonylureas; the carbohydrate content of approximately 13 g/100g (likely representing residual sugars) further disrupts glycaemic control, making flavoured liqueur unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without explicit medical supervision.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol-induced arrhythmia risk, cardiomyopathy, and drug interactions
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular alcohol consumption is associated with increased risk of atrial fibrillation, dilated cardiomyopathy, and hypertriglyceridaemia; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should avoid flavoured liqueur and all alcoholic beverages, particularly those on anticoagulants or antiarrhythmic medications.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol contraindicated with methotrexate and biologics; pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Alcohol is directly pro-inflammatory and is contraindicated with methotrexate (hepatotoxicity risk) and most biologic DMARDs used in rheumatoid arthritis — patients with RA must avoid all alcoholic beverages including flavoured liqueurs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured liqueur is a ready-to-drink alcoholic beverage produced by combining a distilled spirit base with added natural or artificial flavourings and a sweetening agent, resulting in a product with moderate carbohydrate content (~13 g/100g) and relatively low energy density (~53 kcal/100g) compared to undiluted spirits. Common flavour profiles include fruit (citrus, berry, peach), herbal (mint, anise), cream, and nut variants. The texture is typically smooth and syrupy, with a sweet taste profile that masks the underlying alcohol. Clinically, flavoured liqueur carries significant health concerns: alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation (hypoglycaemia risk in diabetics on insulin or sulfonylureas), directly elevates blood pressure (contraindicated in hypertension), increases cardiovascular risk through arrhythmia and cardiomyopathy pathways, and is contraindicated with methotrexate and biologics used in rheumatoid arthritis. The sugar field in the HPB source reports 0 g, which is anomalous — the 13.12 g/100g carbohydrate value likely represents residual sugars. Nutritionally, protein and fat are absent (naturally zero for a spirit-based beverage); energy derives almost entirely from alcohol and residual carbohydrates. Not suitable for diabetics, hypertensive patients, or those with cardiovascular or inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: Is flavoured liqueur suitable for diabetics?
Can I drink liqueur if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in flavoured liqueur?
Can I drink liqueur on methotrexate?
What is the carbohydrate content of liqueur?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D01040001, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The energy, carbohydrate, protein, fat, and sodium values are taken as reported. The anomalous sugar=0 g is preserved with a documented caveat. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for that field only) but all other fields are HPB-sourced.
notes: Zero-macro justification (g4): protein_g is naturally zero — flavoured liqueur is a spirit-based alcoholic beverage containing no protein by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. fat_g is naturally zero — spirit-based liqueur contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. saturated_fat_g and dietary_fibre_g are also zero by formulation for a spirit-based beverage. sugar_g reports 0 g per HPB source, which is anomalous for a liqueur — the carbohydrate value of 13.12 g/100g likely represents residual sugars not fully disaggregated into the sugar field in the HPB record; this value is preserved as reported with this caveat. Negative sentinel values (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for generic flavoured liqueur not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D01040001 or USDA FoodData Central for this generic category as of 2026. GI note: no direct GI measurement found for generic flavoured liqueur in the University of Sydney GI Database; GI of 65 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on sweet alcoholic beverages with comparable carbohydrate content; direct measurement recommended. Dietary flags: halal and kosher status not confirmed — alcohol-containing beverages are generally not halal or kosher; is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false as alcohol production may involve animal-derived fining agents. Ontology note: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code used are best available matches for the liqueur category; FoodEx2 code A00LN (Liqueurs and cordials) is a defensible exact match.
